definition: the process of drawing conclusions about populations, relationships, or causal effects based on observations from samples or empirical data. inference involves reasoning beyond the immediate evidence and is central to hypothesis testing and theory development.
source: Custom entry (user-defined)

definition: bruce archer’s phrase for a ‘third area’ of human knowing, alongside literacy and numeracy — the epistemology of making and doing in design. wroughting refers to knowing how things are brought about (technology), and wrighting to knowing how to do it (craftsmanship). together they frame design as a distinct mode of knowledge, comparable in scope to science and the humanities.
source: Bruce Archer, Time for a Revolution in Art and Design Education, RCA Papers No. 6, 1978

definition: a system treated only by its inputs and outputs while its inner workings are hidden or ignored. in cybernetics, a black box is a situation where the observer has incomplete access to the system’s internal structure (pask). in science & technology studies, “blackboxing” names how successful technical work becomes invisible so only i/o are attended to (latour). in contemporary ai/ml, complex models—especially deep nets—are often described as black-box models because their decision processes are opaque, motivating interpretability and explainability research.
source: G. Pask, “An Approach to Cybernetics,” 1961; B. Latour, Science in Action, 1987; C. Rudin, ‘Stop Explaining Black Box Machine Learning Models,’ 2019; V. Hassija et al., ‘Interpreting Black-Box Models,’ 2024

definition: a sociotechnical approach developed in science and technology studies (sts) that treats both humans and nonhumans (machines, texts, institutions, technologies) as 'actors' in a network. rather than explaining outcomes solely by social forces or technical properties, ant follows how heterogeneous actors form associations, stabilize, and produce effects. bruno latour, michel callon, and john law emphasized that knowledge, power, and technology emerge from these networks, not from isolated human intentions.
source: B. Latour, Science in Action (1987); M. Callon, 'Some Elements of a Sociology of Translation' (1986); J. Law, 'Notes on the Theory of the Actor-Network' (1992)

definition: a structured interface that allows different software systems to communicate by exposing endpoints for data exchange and functionality. In this project, GraphQL APIs expose metadata and presigned media links to clients.
source: Field usage in software engineering; GraphQL specification

definition: a cloud-based authentication and authorization service that issues secure JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for identity verification and access control.
source: Auth0 Documentation, https://auth0.com/docs

definition: a set of software practices where code changes are automatically built, tested, and deployed. GitHub Actions pipelines in this project ensure reliable deployments to DigitalOcean and GitHub Pages.
source: Martin Fowler, 'Continuous Integration' (2006); GitHub Actions docs

definition: a visual representation of data structures showing entities (e.g. artefacts, media, users) and their relationships (e.g. ownership, provenance).
source: Peter Chen, 'The Entity–Relationship Model' (1976)

definition: a binary JSON data type in PostgreSQL that allows efficient storage and querying of structured JSON documents, used here for multilingual metadata fields.
source: PostgreSQL Documentation, JSON Types

definition: a compact, URL-safe token format that encodes claims about a user’s identity and permissions. The backend validates JWTs issued by Auth0 before allowing mutations.
source: IETF RFC 7519

definition: a technology that extracts machine-readable text from scanned images or documents. OCR outputs are embedded for semantic search in the archive.
source: Ray Kurzweil, 'The Age of Intelligent Machines' (1990); OCR technical literature

definition: a method of restricting system access based on assigned roles. Administrators in this platform are given elevated permissions for ingestion and editing.
source: David Ferraiolo & D. Richard Kuhn, 'Role-Based Access Control' (1992)

definition: a formal project management document that defines scope, deliverables, timelines, and acceptance criteria. Here it governs the RCA DDR archive ingestion platform project.
source: Project Management Institute (PMI) standards

definition: the layer of an application where users interact visually with the system. Built here using React and the IBM Carbon Design System.
source: Donald Norman, 'The Design of Everyday Things' (1988)

definition: a testing phase where real users validate the system against requirements before production deployment.
source: ISTQB Glossary of Software Testing Terms

definition: international accessibility standards ensuring digital content is perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust. the frontend complies with WCAG 2.1 AA.
source: W3C Web Accessibility Initiative, WCAG 2.1 (2018)

definition: an international descriptive standard published by the International Council on Archives (ICA) that defines general rules and a common set of elements for creating archival unit descriptions across formats and institutions.
source: ICA, ISAD(G): General International Standard Archival Description – Second Edition (2000)

definition: the entire body of records created and/or accumulated by a particular person, family or organisation in the course of its functions and preserved as an organic whole.
source: Society of American Archivists Dictionary; UNBC Archives 101 Guide

definition: a persistent identifier: a long-lasting, unique reference (often globally resolvable) to a digital object or resource, used to ensure reliable identification and access even when URLs change.
source: Digital Preservation Handbook – Persistent Identifiers section

definition: a cryptographic hash value (using the SHA-256 algorithm) computed over a file or data object to serve as a ‘digital fingerprint’ so that any change to the bit-pattern will produce a different hash; used for integrity verification and fixity checking in digital preservation.
source: Digital Preservation Handbook – Fixity and Checksums; Digital preservation step-by-step

definition: a two-part identifier (type/subtype) formally known as an Internet media type (formerly ‘MIME type’) that indicates the nature and format of a document, file or data stream — used to inform systems how to process or render the content.
source: MDN Web Docs – Media Types; Wikipedia – Media type

definition: information which can be used — alone or in combination with other data — to identify an individual, and which therefore may require redaction or special handling in archival or data-management contexts.
source: Piwik PRO blog – What is PII, non-PII, and personal data?

definition: a metadata element set standard developed by the Visual Resources Association for describing works of visual culture (and the images documenting them) — version 4.0 is expressed in XML and provides a hierarchy of work/image/collection entity types.
source: VRA Core 4.0 – Library of Congress; DCC guidance

definition: the property or assurance that a digital object has remained unchanged over time (i.e. ‘fixed’); often monitored via checksums or other hash values to signal whether any unintended alteration or corruption has occurred.
source: Digital Preservation Handbook; UFS LibGuides – Fixity

definition: a unique identifier assigned by a repository to a group of records or materials at the time they are formally accepted into custody, used to link the physical or digital objects with their acquisition and descriptive documentation.
source: Society of American Archivists Dictionary of Archives Terminology; National Archives and Records Administration description

definition: a high-level conceptual reference model for an archive involving an organisation of people and systems that have accepted responsibility for long-term preservation and access of information for a designated community; it defines functional entities (ingest, archival storage, data management, preservation planning, access, administration) and information packages (SIP, AIP, DIP).
source: ISO 14721:2012 / ISO 14721:2025 standard; Introduction to OAIS Reference Model
